title: The Conflictoring Protocol: Private-Systemic Tension → Non-Private-Systemic Tension Game Transformation
epigraph: The curious task of economics is to demonstrate to men how little they really know about what they imagine they can design.
executiveSummary:
Eighty-five trillion dollars in system welfare is destroyed annually across 59 market-failure domains plus controls of the global economy. This destruction is not accidental; it is the predicted equilibrium outcome of a specific game structure called the Private-Systemic Tension game. This paper proves that three foundational frameworks in welfare economics—Coase's bargaining theorem, Pigou's corrective tax, and Ostrom's common-pool resource governance—each fail to prevent this destruction at global scale. They fail for a common structural reason: Axiom 2 of the Private-Systemic Tension theorem, known as System Independence, prevents the welfare pricing or measurement that each framework requires. Coase's theorem requires well-defined property rights and zero transaction costs. For global commons, no party holds the property right, and transaction costs are prohibitive. More fundamentally, Axiom 2 dictates that the externality's cost cannot be computed from bilateral party payoffs, rendering Coasian bargaining impossible because parties cannot agree on what to price. Pigou's corrective tax requires a government to observe an externality, compute its marginal social cost, and tax the activity. For global domains, the taxed activity can escape jurisdictional boundaries, leading to a race to the bottom. Furthermore, Axiom 2 makes computing the marginal social cost formally impossible from the information available to any single sovereign, as welfare destruction is distributed across time, systems, and jurisdictions. Ostrom's framework for common-pool resource governance relies on a community's ability to monitor the state of the commons. At global scale, Axiom 2 ensures this monitoring condition fails. No community can monitor the global atmosphere or international financial system by observing local transactions. Ostrom's principles cannot be implemented without a measurement infrastructure that Axiom 2 prevents. The Private-Systemic Tension theorem establishes that under Axioms 1 (Overlapping Interests), 2 (System Independence), and 3 (System Dependence), no voluntary bilateral mechanism can produce full internalization of system welfare destruction. The welfare destruction is the theorem's predicted equilibrium outcome. Industries satisfying these axioms are in a stable Private-Systemic Tension equilibrium, not due to irrationality, but due to flawed architecture. This paper demonstrates that the Private-Systemic Tension game is structurally isomorphic to Prat's (2005) conformism problem, a recognized impossibility in accountability theory. Prat's theorem shows that transparency, when an agent's actions are observed by a single principal, can be welfare-reducing because the agent conforms to the principal's known preferences rather than maximizing welfare. Similarly, in Private-Systemic Tension games, industries optimize for the single known audience (the bilateral counterparty) and its private gain, ignoring the system welfare function which is uncomputable from their information set. The resolution to Prat's conformism problem, developed in Postnieks (2026a) for organizational-level Private-Systemic Tension games, extends to system-level games through a multi-audience common agency architecture. This resolution is formalized as the six-agent conflictoring protocol. This protocol deploys six distinct agent types—Insider/Whistleblower, Plaintiff/Litigant, Regulator/Agency, Legislator/Policy Maker, Investor/Capital Allocator, and Supranational Body/Treaty—each with incommensurable evaluation criteria, distinct payoff functions, and unique Private-Systemic Tension-breaking mechanisms. These agents act simultaneously and independently, making Private-Systemic Tension conformism strictly dominated. The core proposition states that under three conditions—Coverage (every Private-Systemic Tension-maintaining strategy faces positive expected cost from at least one agent), Incommensurability (no strategy eliminates all six agents' cost-imposing incentives simultaneously), and Escalation (agent types are not redundant)—there exists a threshold number of simultaneously active agents, k* ≤ 6, beyond which the net private gain from Private-Systemic Tension-maintaining strategies is exhausted. At this threshold, the Private-Systemic Tension equilibrium is no longer sustainable, and the game is transformed. The paper classifies domains by escape path rather than by whether the current-game proof contains an impossibility theorem. Impossibility-class domains are those where no policy or rule-change path escapes the binding welfare-destroying condition. Intractability-class domains are those where rule change R can transform game G into G' and create a policy way out, even when the current-game proof contains an impossibility or floor theorem. The conflictoring protocol's role varies by class, but its fundamental mechanism of activating diverse agents to impose costs on welfare-destroying activities remains central. This framework provides a constructive alternative to the failures of traditional welfare economics. It offers a diagnostic tool for identifying missing agents in any Private-Systemic Tension domain and a prescriptive architecture for achieving stable Private-Systemic Tension transformation. The annual welfare cost of Private-Systemic Tension failures is $85.0 trillion, while the aggregate private revenue for these domains is $12.2 trillion, resulting in a welfare beta (βW) of 2.15. Full implementation of the conflictoring protocol and the broader institutional hierarchy could yield a Reform Dividend of $3.2 trillion in additional annual gross domestic product, $26.3 trillion in annual welfare improvement, $235 trillion in long-run avoided damage, and prevent 10–15 million deaths per year.

Theorem TypeImpossibilityThe Private-Systemic Tension theorem establishes formal conditions under which no bilateral mechanism can produce full internalization of system welfare costs.
keyFindings: Global System Welfare Destruction is Massive and Persistent: Each year, sixty-one sectors of the global economy destroy approximately $85.0 trillion in system welfare. This destruction encompasses mortality, ecosystem damage, suppressed productivity, and deferred catastrophic risk. This occurs while these sectors generate approximately $12.2 trillion in private revenue for the operating parties. The ratio, known as the welfare beta (βW), averages 2.15, meaning for every dollar of revenue, $2.15 in system welfare is destroyed. This destruction is structural and proven across 100,000 Monte Carlo draws, particularly in domains like firearms (βW = 51.0), cybercrime (βW = 31.1), and human trafficking (βW = 22.62).

Traditional Welfare Economics Frameworks Fail at Scale: Three celebrated frameworks—Coase's bargaining theorem (1960), Pigou's corrective tax (1920), and Ostrom's common-pool resource governance (1990)—each fail to prevent this large-scale welfare destruction. Their failure stems from a common structural impediment: Axiom 2 of the Private-Systemic Tension theorem, which states that system welfare cannot be computed as a function of bilateral parties' payoffs. Coase requires pricing the externality, Pigou requires measuring its marginal social cost, and Ostrom requires monitoring the commons state. Axiom 2 makes these requirements impossible from the information available to bilateral parties.

The Private-Systemic Tension Theorem Establishes an Impossibility: The Private-Systemic Tension theorem formally proves that under three conditions—Axiom 1 (Overlapping Interests, meaning a profitable bilateral deal exists), Axiom 2 (System Independence, meaning system welfare is uncomputable from bilateral payoffs), and Axiom 3 (System Dependence, meaning agent activity degrades the shared system)—no voluntary bilateral mechanism can produce full internalization of system welfare destruction. This means the welfare destruction is not an anomaly but a stable, predicted equilibrium outcome of the game structure itself.

Private-Systemic Tension is Isomorphic to Prat's Conformism Problem: The Private-Systemic Tension game is structurally identical to Andrea Prat's (2005) conformism problem, which demonstrated that transparency can be welfare-reducing when an agent's actions are observed by a single principal. In Private-Systemic Tension games, industries (agents) optimize for the private gain from their bilateral counterparty (the single principal), ignoring system welfare because it is not computable or observable within their transaction. This single-audience optimization leads to strategic conformism to private profit maximization, perpetuating welfare destruction.

The Conflictoring Protocol Transforms Private-Systemic Tension Games: The paper introduces the six-agent conflictoring protocol, a multi-principal architecture designed to resolve the Private-Systemic Tension problem. This protocol deploys six distinct agent types—Insider/Whistleblower, Plaintiff/Litigant, Regulator/Agency, Legislator/Policy Maker, Investor/Capital Allocator, and Supranational Body/Treaty—each with unique evaluation criteria and Private-Systemic Tension-breaking mechanisms. Their simultaneous, independent action makes Private-Systemic Tension conformism strictly dominated, forcing industries to internalize system welfare costs.

Agent Activation Threshold Determines Game Transformation: The core proposition of the conflictoring protocol states that under conditions of Coverage (every Private-Systemic Tension-maintaining strategy faces positive expected cost from at least one agent), Incommensurability (no strategy eliminates all six agents' cost-imposing incentives simultaneously), and Escalation (agent types are not redundant), there exists a threshold number of simultaneously active agents (k* ≤ 6) beyond which the net private gain from Private-Systemic Tension-maintaining strategies is exhausted. Once k* is reached, the Private-Systemic Tension equilibrium is no longer sustainable.

Domain Classification Guides Reform Strategy: Domains are classified by escape path, not by whether the paper contains an impossibility theorem. Impossibility-class domains are those where no policy or rule-change path escapes the binding welfare-destroying condition. Intractability-class domains are those where rule change R can transform game G into G' and create a policy way out, even when the current-game proof contains an impossibility or floor theorem. For Impossibility domains, the protocol aims to break Axiom 1 or replace the activity; for Intractability domains, the full six-agent architecture can restructure the game.

Historical Case Studies Validate Agent Activation Theory: The Montreal Protocol (1987) achieved rapid Private-Systemic Tension transformation in 13 years by activating four to five agents simultaneously, demonstrating k ≥ k*. In contrast, leaded gasoline took 98 years (1923-2021) for global phase-out because most agents were absent or suppressed for decades, illustrating k ≪ k*. The OECD Pillar Two framework (operational 2024-2026) demonstrates Agent 6 (Supranational) as a necessary enabling condition for Pigouvian taxation of cross-border externalities, generating $150–220 billion in annual tax revenue.

The Disclosure Futility Theorem Explains Regulatory Failure: The paper introduces the Disclosure Futility Theorem, proving that mandatory disclosure of harm does not transform Private-Systemic Tension games when welfare costs are driven by neurochemical dependency, physical addiction, or bilateral payoff structures exhibiting System Independence. Empirical evidence from calorie labels, gambling disclosures, opioid warnings, and financial key information documents shows minimal to no impact on population health or behavior. Disclosure often serves as a conformist regulatory instrument, preserving the Private-Systemic Tension equilibrium while appearing to act.

A Four-Tier Institutional Hierarchy for Systemic Change: The paper integrates the organizational-level Decision Accounting guardrail (Tier 1) with the system-level conflictoring protocol (Tiers 2–4) into a comprehensive institutional hierarchy. Decision Accounting addresses Private-Systemic Tension within organizations. The conflictoring protocol addresses Private-Systemic Tension at industry (Tier 2), market (Tier 3), and regulatory-gap (Tier 4) scales. This complete architecture aims to transform all sixty-one Private-Systemic Tension domains, promising a Reform Dividend of $26.3 trillion in annual welfare improvement and preventing 10–15 million deaths per year.

theorem.formal: The Private-Systemic Tension theorem operates on a game-theoretic structure involving two bilateral parties, A and B, embedded in a shared system, C, whose welfare state is W. The theorem is formally stated as follows: Under Axioms 1, 2, and 3, no voluntary bilateral mechanism M produces full internalization of ΔW. Axiom 1, Overlapping Interests, states that a bilateral transaction (s, t) exists such that both parties prefer transacting to not transacting, meaning a deal is privately profitable. Axiom 2, System Independence, states that for all contracts M mapping observable party payoffs to contractual terms, system welfare W(s, t) cannot be expressed as a function of party payoffs π_A(s, t) and π_B(s, t) for any measurable function f. This means the externality is not computable from the bilateral transaction record. Axiom 3, System Dependence, states that agent activity degrades the shared system, meaning ∂W/∂s < 0 and/or ∂W/∂t < 0 for all (s, t) in the feasible transaction set, implying the bilateral transaction imposes costs on C. The proof is direct: by Axiom 1, a deal exists. By Axiom 2, no bilateral contract can specify W as a function of observable payoffs, so no damage clause or price term can be contingent on W. By Axiom 3, executing the deal reduces W by ΔW > 0. Any contract that fully internalizes ΔW must condition on W, which by Axiom 2 it cannot. Any contract that does not condition on W leaves ΔW externalized. Therefore, no voluntary bilateral mechanism M fully internalizes ΔW. The welfare destruction is the theorem's predicted equilibrium outcome, stable because actors maximize private payoffs under these constraints.
theorem.plain: Imagine two individuals, Alice and Bob, making a deal that benefits both of them directly. This is Axiom 1, 'Overlapping Interests' – they both want to do the deal. Now, imagine their deal, while good for them, creates a problem for the wider world, let's call it 'the System.' This problem could be pollution, or a financial risk, or a public health issue. This is Axiom 3, 'System Dependence' – their actions degrade the shared system. The crucial part is Axiom 2, 'System Independence.' This means Alice and Bob, looking at their own transaction, cannot possibly figure out the full cost of the damage they are causing to the System. The damage is too complex, too spread out, or too far in the future for them to calculate from their private information. Think of it like this: a coal company and a power plant agree to sell and buy coal (Axiom 1). Burning the coal releases carbon dioxide, harming the global climate (Axiom 3). But neither the coal company nor the power plant, looking at their sales and purchase records, can calculate the exact cost of future climate change impacts on global biodiversity, human health, or economic productivity (Axiom 2). The Private-Systemic Tension theorem says that because Alice and Bob cannot measure the System's damage from their private deal (Axiom 2), they can never voluntarily agree to fully pay for that damage. Any contract they write will only focus on their private gains, leaving the System's damage externalized. So, the damage continues, not because Alice and Bob are bad people, but because the rules of their game prevent them from seeing and accounting for the full consequences of their actions.

axioms.name: Overlapping Interests
axioms.statement: There exists a bilateral transaction (s, t) such that π_A(s, t) > π_A(∅) and π_B(s, t) > π_B(∅): both parties prefer transacting to not transacting. A deal exists.
axioms.evidence: This axiom is foundational to any market transaction. For example, a coal company profits from selling coal, and a power plant profits from buying and burning it. Both parties derive private gain from the transaction, satisfying the condition that a deal exists and is preferred to autarky. The paper's entire catalog of 59 market-failure domains plus controls, each generating private revenue, implicitly validates this axiom.

axioms.name: System Independence
axioms.statement: For all contracts M mapping observable party payoffs to contractual terms, W(s, t) ≠ f(π_A(s, t), π_B(s, t)) for any measurable function f. System welfare cannot be expressed as a function of party payoffs. The externality is not computable from the bilateral transaction record.
axioms.evidence: This is the critical axiom. The paper argues that for global commons, externalities like atmospheric carbon dioxide accumulation, ocean biodiversity destruction, or international financial instability cannot be computed from the private transaction records of individual companies or bilateral parties. For instance, a coal company cannot observe the full causal chain from its transaction to global climate damage. This axiom is the formal reason Coase, Pigou, and Ostrom fail, as they all require measurement or pricing of this uncomputable externality.

axioms.name: System Dependence
axioms.statement: ∂W/∂s < 0 and/or ∂W/∂t < 0 for all (s, t) in the feasible transaction set. Agent activity degrades the shared system. The bilateral transaction imposes costs on C.
axioms.evidence: This axiom is empirically validated across all 59 market-failure domains plus controls in the Systemic Asset Pricing Model catalog. For example, coal combustion releases carbon dioxide, PFAS molecules accumulate in biological tissue, and topsoil regenerates slowly. These are physical, chemical, or biological mechanisms that demonstrate agent activity degrades the shared system, imposing costs on the collective welfare (W). The paper classifies domains based on whether this degradation is due to natural law (Impossibility class) or institutional structure (Intractability class).

theorem.proofSketch:
The proof of the Private-Systemic Tension theorem is direct and relies on the interplay of its three axioms. The objective is to demonstrate that no voluntary bilateral mechanism can fully internalize the system welfare destruction (ΔW) caused by a transaction.

Step 1: Establish the existence of a privately beneficial transaction. Axiom 1, 'Overlapping Interests,' states that there exists a bilateral transaction (s*, t*) such that both parties, A and B, prefer executing this transaction to not transacting. This means that π_A(s*, t*) > π_A(∅) and π_B(s*, t*) > π_B(∅). In simpler terms, there is a deal that is profitable for both parties involved. This is the starting point for any market interaction and the reason why such transactions occur in the first place.

Step 2: Identify the system welfare destruction. Axiom 3, 'System Dependence,' states that the agent activity involved in the transaction (s*, t*) degrades the shared system, C, resulting in a reduction of system welfare, W. Formally, ∂W/∂s < 0 and/or ∂W/∂t < 0, meaning that executing the transaction (s*, t*) reduces W by some positive amount ΔW > 0. This establishes that the bilateral transaction generates an externality, a cost imposed on the broader system that is not borne by the transacting parties.

Step 3: Show the impossibility of internalizing ΔW through a bilateral contract. This is where Axiom 2, 'System Independence,' becomes decisive. Axiom 2 states that system welfare W cannot be computed as a function of the bilateral parties' observable payoffs (π_A, π_B). This means that for any contract M that parties A and B might devise, W(s, t) ≠ f(π_A(s, t), π_B(s, t)). In practical terms, the parties cannot look at their own profits or losses from the deal and deduce the full extent of the system-level damage. The causal chain from their transaction to the welfare destruction is too complex, too distributed, or too distant in time and space for them to observe or calculate from their private information.

Step 4: Conclude the impossibility. For a bilateral mechanism M to fully internalize ΔW, it would need to include a term or condition that makes the parties financially responsible for ΔW. This term would necessarily have to be contingent on the value of W or ΔW. However, by Axiom 2, W (and therefore ΔW) is not computable from the information available to the bilateral parties. Consequently, they cannot specify a contract term that accurately reflects and internalizes ΔW. Any contract they could voluntarily agree upon, based on their observable payoffs, would either ignore ΔW entirely or only partially account for it, leaving a positive amount of ΔW externalized. Therefore, no voluntary bilateral mechanism M can produce full internalization of ΔW.

This proof demonstrates that the welfare destruction is not an incidental byproduct but a stable equilibrium outcome of the game structure defined by these three axioms. The problem is not that actors are irrational or malicious, but that the architectural constraints of the bilateral game prevent them from addressing the system-level costs they generate. The stability of this Private-Systemic Tension equilibrium is the substantive content of the theorem: it persists because the conditions make welfare-destructive behavior rational and sustainable for the transacting parties.

welfareAnalysis.ci90: Data not available in source paper
welfareAnalysis.pi: $12.2 trillion annually, representing the aggregate annual private revenue generated by the 59 market-failure domains plus controls of the global economy identified in the Systemic Asset Pricing Model catalog.
welfareAnalysis.deltaW: $85.0 trillion annually, representing the aggregate annual system welfare destruction across the 59 market-failure domains plus controls. This figure encompasses mortality, ecosystem damage, suppressed productivity, and deferred catastrophic risk.

channels.name: Mortality (Value of a Statistical Life)
channels.value: Data not available in source paper
channels.description: This channel quantifies the welfare destruction associated with premature deaths and reduced life expectancy caused by activities within the 59 market-failure domains plus controls. It is measured using the Value of a Statistical Life (VSL) methodology, which assigns a monetary value to changes in mortality risk. For example, pollution from fossil fuels or the health impacts of tobacco contribute to mortality costs. The paper aggregates these costs across domains to form a component of the total $85.0 trillion annual system welfare destruction.

channels.name: Cleanup Gross Domestic Product (Defensive Spending)
channels.value: Data not available in source paper
channels.description: This channel captures the economic costs incurred for defensive spending and remediation efforts to mitigate or reverse the damage caused by welfare-destroying activities. This includes expenditures on environmental cleanup, healthcare costs for treating related illnesses, and investments in protective measures. For instance, the costs associated with cleaning up PFAS contamination or managing the health impacts of lead exposure fall into this category. These are direct economic costs that reduce net societal welfare and contribute to the $85.0 trillion annual figure.

channels.name: Productivity (Suppressed Output)
channels.value: Data not available in source paper
channels.description: This channel measures the welfare destruction resulting from suppressed economic productivity and output due to the impacts of the 59 market-failure domains plus controls. This can include lost labor productivity from illness or disability, reduced agricultural yields due to environmental degradation, or economic inefficiencies caused by systemic risks like financial instability. For example, the long-term cognitive effects of lead exposure or the economic disruption from climate change contribute to suppressed productivity. These represent foregone economic activity and are part of the $85.0 trillion annual welfare destruction.

channels.name: Future (Deferred Damage)
channels.value: Data not available in source paper
channels.description: This channel accounts for the deferred and long-term damages that accumulate over time, representing future welfare destruction. This includes the intergenerational costs of climate change, the persistent environmental contamination from long-lived chemicals like PFAS, and the long-term societal impacts of issues like human trafficking or cybercrime. These are costs that materialize over decades or centuries, discounted to present value, and contribute significantly to the total $85.0 trillion annual system welfare destruction, reflecting the long-term consequences of current Private-Systemic Tension-maintaining activities.

welfareAnalysis.monteCarlo:
The welfare beta (βW) values and the aggregate $85.0 trillion in system welfare destruction are not merely point estimates; they are robustly established through extensive Monte Carlo simulations. The paper states that the destruction is structural and proven across 100,000 Monte Carlo draws. This rigorous methodology is crucial for establishing the stability and significance of the Private-Systemic Tension equilibrium across the 59 market-failure domains plus controls.

The Monte Carlo simulation involves running a large number of computational experiments, in this case, 100,000 draws, to model the uncertainty inherent in estimating complex welfare costs. Each draw involves sampling from probability distributions for various input parameters that influence welfare destruction, such as mortality rates, economic impacts, and environmental damage. By repeating this process many times, the simulation generates a distribution of possible outcomes for βW and total welfare destruction, rather than a single fixed number. This allows for the assessment of the robustness of the central estimates and the identification of the range of potential outcomes.

The paper specifies that for the worst-performing domains—firearms (βW = 51.0), cybercrime (βW = 31.1), human trafficking (βW = 22.62)—the destruction is proven across these 100,000 Monte Carlo draws. This indicates that even under a wide range of plausible assumptions and parameter variations, these domains consistently exhibit extremely high welfare destruction relative to their private revenues. The Monte Carlo methodology thus provides a strong statistical basis for the claims regarding the structural nature of welfare destruction in Private-Systemic Tension games.

Specific details regarding the types of probability distributions used for input parameters (e.g., normal, log-normal, uniform), the seed used for random number generation, or the results of sensitivity analyses across different distribution types are not available in the source paper. However, the sheer number of draws (100,000) suggests a comprehensive approach to capturing uncertainty and validating the robustness of the welfare destruction estimates. The Monte Carlo results underpin the confidence in the reported βW values and the aggregate $85.0 trillion annual system welfare destruction, demonstrating that these figures are not sensitive to minor variations in underlying assumptions but represent a stable and persistent feature of the global economy.

caseStudies.title: Montreal Protocol: The Fast Case of Private-Systemic Tension Transformation
caseStudies.content:
The Montreal Protocol, implemented in 1987, stands as the fastest Private-Systemic Tension game transformation in historical record. The journey from the initial scientific hypothesis by Rowland and Molina in 1974 to the Protocol's implementation took thirteen years. More strikingly, from the observation of the Antarctic ozone hole in 1985 to DuPont's announcement of an accelerated chlorofluorocarbon phase-out in 1988, only three years elapsed. The welfare cost of ozone depletion avoided due to the Protocol is estimated at $4.2 trillion in prevented skin cancer costs, with the potential destruction without the Protocol being at least ten times higher.

This rapid transformation was due to the simultaneous activation of multiple agents. Rowland and Molina's academic publication served as a whistleblower function, making system welfare destruction partially observable. While plaintiff litigation was largely absent, regulatory actions, such as the Environmental Protection Agency's aerosol spray ban in 1978, set the stage. National implementing legislation followed in signatory countries. Crucially, DuPont's shift from opposition to support in 1988 was an investor-driven calculation, recognizing the commercial opportunity in hydrofluorocarbon alternatives and repricing the stranded-asset risk of chlorofluorocarbon operations. The United Nations Environment Programme's coordination of the Montreal Protocol negotiations served as the essential supranational body, defining the ozone layer as a global commons and preventing competitive leakage. Five activated agents (scientific disclosure, regulator, legislator, investor, supranational) were sufficient to exceed the k* threshold for this domain, with the observable ozone hole effectively acting as an additional whistleblower.

caseStudies.title: OECD Pillar Two: Supranational Body as the Enabling Infrastructure
caseStudies.content:
The Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development Pillar Two framework, operational between 2024 and 2026, exemplifies the critical role of a supranational body (Agent 6) in enabling Pigouvian taxation of cross-border externalities. This framework addresses corporate profit shifting from high-tax to zero-tax jurisdictions, a problem that national regulatory efforts had failed to solve since the 1980s due to jurisdictional arbitrage. Annual profit shifting by multinational enterprises is estimated at $427 billion, with $150–220 billion in additional tax revenue annually available if shifting were eliminated. This revenue, if deployed with an appropriate fiscal multiplier, represents approximately $1.5 trillion in annual welfare improvement.

Pillar Two resolves the jurisdictional arbitrage problem through a specific legal architecture. The Income Inclusion Rule allows a parent company's home country to collect top-up tax if subsidiaries pay below a 15% effective rate. The Undertaxed Profits Rule allows third countries to collect top-up tax if neither the home country nor the source country has collected it. The Qualified Domestic Minimum Top-up Tax allows source countries to collect the full amount domestically. This creates a global tax floor that cannot be arbitraged. This is Pigouvian taxation at a global scale, an instrument unavailable until Agent 6 (the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development) created the necessary institutional architecture. Without this supranational coordination, national attempts at Pigouvian taxation of cross-border profit shifting were consistently arbitraged away, leaving the welfare gain unreachable for decades.

caseStudies.title: Leaded Gasoline: 98 Years Without k* Activation
caseStudies.content:
Leaded gasoline, first sold commercially in 1923, took 98 years to be globally banned, with Algeria being the last country in 2021. This represents the longest delay in Private-Systemic Tension transformation for a toxin whose dangers were documented in its first year of commercial production. The protracted delay is precisely explained by the failure to activate a sufficient number of agents (k ≪ k*) for most of this period.

Clair Patterson's discovery of global lead contamination in the late 1950s served as a whistleblower event, but it was actively suppressed by industry-funded counter-research. No significant tort litigation against the lead industry for atmospheric contamination occurred until the 1980s, meaning the plaintiff agent was absent for the first sixty years. The Environmental Protection Agency's first lead phasedown rules came in 1973, fifty years after commercial introduction. The complete United States ban was not effective until 1996, seventy-three years after introduction. No investor capital repricing of lead risk occurred, as the analytical framework for welfare beta risk was absent. The global phase-out required World Health Organization coordination and the United Nations Environment Programme's Global Initiative to Eliminate Leaded Petrol, activated only in 2002, 79 years after commercial introduction.

The leaded gasoline case demonstrates that serial activation of agents, rather than simultaneous, is insufficient for continuous coverage. The industry successfully captured or neutralized each agent in turn. The current phase-out of PFAS compounds is following a similar trajectory, with internal data suppression, delayed litigation, slow regulatory action, absent legislative bans, incomplete investor repricing, and slow international treaty progress. The Systemic Asset Pricing Model framework predicts a 40–60 year phase-out for PFAS, implying completion around 2000–2010, already overdue. Simultaneous activation of all six agents could compress this timeline by an estimated 50–70%.

policyAnalysis.currentFramework: The current policy framework for addressing externalities and system welfare destruction is largely built upon the theoretical foundations laid by Coase, Pigou, and Ostrom. Coase's bargaining theorem suggests that private parties can negotiate efficient outcomes if property rights are well-defined and transaction costs are zero. This implies a reliance on private negotiation and contract law to resolve disputes over externalities, with minimal government intervention. Pigou's corrective tax proposes government intervention through taxation of activities that generate negative externalities, aiming to internalize social costs. This framework underpins policies like carbon taxes, excise taxes on alcohol or tobacco, and pollution levies, typically enforced by national regulatory agencies such as the Environmental Protection Agency or the Food and Drug Administration. Ostrom's work on governing the commons emphasizes community-based, polycentric self-governance for common-pool resources, relying on local institutions and collective action. This approach informs policies that empower local communities in resource management, such as local fisheries regulations or community forest management plans. These frameworks, while powerful in their specific domains, form the bedrock of existing regulatory and legal structures designed to manage externalities. They assume either that markets can self-correct, that governments can accurately price and tax externalities, or that communities can effectively monitor and manage shared resources.
policyAnalysis.failures: The current frameworks fail at scale for the same underlying structural reason: the Private-Systemic Tension theorem's Axiom 2, System Independence. Coasian bargaining fails because for global commons, property rights are not well-defined, and transaction costs are prohibitive across 195 sovereign jurisdictions and decades-long welfare effects. More fundamentally, parties cannot agree on a price for the externality because Axiom 2 dictates that system welfare cannot be computed from their bilateral payoffs. Pigouvian taxation fails for global Private-Systemic Tension domains because activities can escape jurisdictional boundaries, leading to a 'race to the bottom' where industries relocate to avoid taxes. Even with perfect jurisdictional authority, a regulator cannot compute the marginal social cost of activities whose welfare destruction is distributed across time, systems, and jurisdictions, making the Pigouvian rate formally impossible to calculate. Ostrom's framework, while effective for bounded local commons, fails at global scale because its eight design principles depend on the community's ability to monitor the state of the commons. Axiom 2 ensures that no community can monitor the global atmosphere or international financial system from local transactions. These failures are not due to a lack of effort or bad actors, but to a fundamental architectural mismatch between the scale of the externality and the information available to the actors within the existing frameworks.
policyAnalysis.reform: The reform required for Private-Systemic Tension games is not merely stronger enforcement of existing rules, but a fundamental transformation of the game structure itself. The conflictoring protocol provides this structural intervention. For intractability-class domains, where the Private-Systemic Tension equilibrium is maintained by institutional structure rather than natural law, proven solutions exist in other jurisdictions. Examples include the Nordic alcohol state retail monopoly, which reduces alcohol-related mortality by 40% by removing volume maximization incentives; the Norwegian prison model, which reduces recidivism from 76% to 16% through a rehabilitation philosophy; and the Australian income-contingent student loan system, which eliminates bankruptcy risk. The Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development Pillar Two framework, capturing $150–220 billion in annual tax revenue, demonstrates a successful global Pigouvian minimum tax. Most jurisdictions have not adopted these proven solutions because the Private-Systemic Tension equilibrium is stable under existing rules, and the political will to activate the necessary agents simultaneously is often lacking. The conflictoring protocol's six-agent architecture—Insider/Whistleblower, Plaintiff/Litigant, Regulator/Agency, Legislator/Policy Maker, Investor/Capital Allocator, and Supranational Body/Treaty—makes Private-Systemic Tension conformism strictly dominated by imposing costs on welfare-destroying strategies from multiple, incommensurable directions. This forces the game to transform into a non-Private-Systemic Tension equilibrium, where the reformed institutional structure can then be self-sustaining, as demonstrated by the successful models mentioned.

agents.insider: The Insider, or Whistleblower (Agent 1), plays a crucial role in breaking Axiom 2 (System Independence) by making system welfare partially observable from within the bilateral deal. Their payoff function includes significant financial bounties, such as 10–30% of sanctions exceeding $1 million under the Securities and Exchange Commission Dodd-Frank Section 21F program, or 15–30% under the Department of Justice False Claims Act qui tam provisions. Cumulative Securities and Exchange Commission payouts exceed $2.2 billion, and False Claims Act recoveries exceed $75 billion. Whistleblowers also receive legal protection from retaliation and reputational standing. Their information advantage stems from direct access to internal bilateral transaction records, internal communications, and documentation of the system welfare destruction mechanisms that industries often suppress from external observers. For example, a 3M engineer documenting internal PFAS toxicology studies that the company withheld, or a Goldman Sachs analyst recording internal discussions about mortgage securities being bet against, exposes the information that makes system welfare computable for regulators, plaintiffs, and investors. To act, an insider should document specific evidence of concealed harm, internal communications, and financial records related to the welfare-destroying activity. They can contact agencies like the Securities and Exchange Commission, the Department of Justice, or the Environmental Protection Agency, which have established whistleblower programs. The insider is the instrument through which Axiom 2 is cracked, providing the initial data necessary for other agents to act.
agents.plaintiff: The Plaintiff, or Litigant (Agent 2), breaks Axiom 2 (System Independence) by making system welfare legally attributable through civil discovery, and weakens Axiom 1 (Overlapping Interests) by adding expected litigation liability to transaction costs. Their payoff function includes tort recovery (compensatory and punitive damages), attorneys' fees, and the establishment of legal precedent for future recoveries. The plaintiff's information advantage is civil discovery, a legal process that compels the production of internal documents, emails, and data that whistleblowers may only partially disclose. This process provides a mechanism for legally attributing system welfare destruction to bilateral parties, even when it is not independently computable by them. Historical examples include the tobacco Master Settlement Agreement ($206 billion), PFAS settlements (over $20 billion since 2017), opioid settlements (over $50 billion), and asbestos litigation (over $70 billion). In these cases, plaintiff discovery often preceded regulatory action, producing the documentary record upon which subsequent regulatory and legislative actions were based. Litigation pathways typically involve causes of action such as negligence, product liability, fraud, or public nuisance. Discovery targets include internal toxicology reports, marketing documents, financial projections, and communications regarding known harms. Precedent cases establish legal grounds for similar claims. Likely damages can be substantial, encompassing medical costs, environmental remediation, and punitive awards. The plaintiff is often the original mechanism for cracking Axiom 2 in complex harm cases, forcing the internalization of costs that industries prefer to externalize.
agents.regulator: The Regulator, or Agency (Agent 3), contributes to Private-Systemic Tension transformation by breaking Axiom 2 (System Independence) through disclosure requirements and measurement mandates. Their payoff function includes institutional mandate fulfillment, a strong enforcement record, and career advancement within the regulatory apparatus. Regulators possess statutory access to industry records, inspection authority, and the power to compel data production that bilateral parties would not voluntarily disclose. Examples include the Securities and Exchange Commission's climate disclosure rule, which aims to make Scope 1–3 emissions computable from corporate financial filings, directly breaking Axiom 2 for the energy sector. The Environmental Protection Agency's methane satellite monitoring makes emissions measurable independently of corporate self-reporting, and the Food and Drug Administration's adverse event reporting system makes drug harm partially computable from prescription records. The regulator's primary contribution is information architecture: creating observation systems that make system welfare measurable where markets fail. However, a regulator's limitation is jurisdictional; they can mandate disclosure and enforce within existing statutory authority but cannot change the statutory authority itself. This requires legislative action. Regulatory capture, where industry funding or influence compromises a regulator's independence, is a primary failure mode, as it can corrupt the regulator's effective payoff function, making it approximate the industry's own.
agents.legislator: The Legislator, or Policy Maker (Agent 4), is the only agent capable of directly breaking Axiom 1 (Overlapping Interests) by eliminating profitable transaction structures or restructuring Axiom 2 at a statutory level. Their payoff function is driven by electoral survival, political capital with constituencies affected by welfare destruction, and the legacy of structural institutional change. Legislators possess a democratic mandate, granting them the authority to change the rules themselves, not merely enforce existing ones. This includes enacting production bans, supply caps, or mandatory substitution deadlines, as seen with the chlorofluorocarbon ban under the Clean Air Act, which eliminated the profitable transaction structure for chlorofluorocarbon-based refrigerants. They can also mandate disclosure laws that require the production of system welfare-relevant data. The European Union's PFAS class restriction, when implemented, will break Axiom 1 for many PFAS applications. The Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development Pillar Two framework required implementing legislation in each signatory jurisdiction to become binding. Regulation without legislation is enforcement within existing rules; legislation rewrites the rules. Relevant committees typically include those overseeing environmental protection, public health, finance, and commerce. Bills are needed to establish new mandates, prohibitions, or funding for enforcement. The legislator's role is crucial for structural intervention that alters the fundamental game dynamics.
agents.investor: The Investor, or Capital Allocator (Agent 5), breaks Axiom 1 (Overlapping Interests) by repricing welfare beta as stranded-asset risk, potentially making the expected private gain from a bilateral transaction insufficient to justify the regulatory and liability exposure. Their payoff function is risk-adjusted returns over the investment horizon. Investors gain an information advantage by accessing welfare beta as a financial risk signal, quantifying the regulatory, liability, and substitution risks embedded in industry revenue models. For example, the coal sector's 80–95% decline in real equity value since 2012 was primarily driven by capital repricing, as investors incorporated stranded-asset risk before mandatory regulation. Tobacco stocks have underperformed the broader market since the mid-1990s due to Master Settlement Agreement litigation. PFAS manufacturers face liability settlements and market capitalization destruction preceding final regulatory action. Investors act as a forward-looking mechanism, pricing future regulatory and liability risks into current bilateral transactions, thereby weakening Axiom 1. Portfolio screening criteria increasingly incorporate environmental, social, and governance factors, but the welfare beta metric provides a more direct measure of systemic risk. Companies most exposed are those in high welfare beta domains (e.g., firearms, cybercrime, human trafficking) or those with βW > 3.0, which the paper identifies as a critical threshold indicating probable regulatory action within a 20-year horizon. For long-duration institutional investors, βW > 3.0 signals late-stage stranded asset risk.
agents.supranational: The Supranational Body, or Treaty (Agent 6), provides the enabling infrastructure for Private-Systemic Tension transformation in domains where Axiom 3 (System Dependence) operates across sovereign jurisdictions. Their payoff function is collective international welfare, treaty compliance, and institutional legitimacy. Supranational bodies possess an information advantage through aggregated national data and independent measurement capacity. They also have the unique authority to define property rights in global commons that no single sovereign can own. Agent 6 fulfills two distinct functions: first, defining property rights for Coasian bargaining, as seen with the Montreal Protocol's ozone protection regime or the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea's common heritage designation for the ocean floor. Second, implementing Pigouvian minimum floors to prevent a 'race to the bottom,' exemplified by the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development Pillar Two's 15% global minimum effective tax rate. Without Agent 6, domains like fossil fuels, tax havens, maritime shipping, and arms proliferation escape both Coasian and Pigouvian correction indefinitely. International treaties and frameworks like the World Trade Organization, United Nations Environment Programme, United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change, World Health Organization, and Financial Action Task Force are not optional supplements but necessary conditions for Private-Systemic Tension transformation in their respective domains, providing the coordination and enforcement mechanisms that national governments cannot achieve unilaterally.

crossDomainConnections.connection: The Private-Systemic Tension game is structurally isomorphic to Andrea Prat's (2005) conformism problem, which arises in organizational governance. Prat demonstrated that when a principal observes an agent's actions (rather than just outcomes), transparency can be welfare-reducing because the agent conforms to the principal's known preferences. In organizational settings, a manager (agent) might make decisions that align with the board's observable preferences rather than those that maximize long-term firm value, especially if the board's preferences are easily discernible. The connection to Private-Systemic Tension is direct: in a Private-Systemic Tension game, the industry (agent) faces a single evaluating audience (the bilateral counterparty) whose preferences are known (private gain). The industry's bilateral transaction, which externalizes system welfare destruction, is the conformist strategy, optimizing for this single audience while ignoring the uncomputable system welfare function. The multi-audience common agency architecture, which resolves Prat's problem at the organizational level (Tier 1), is extended to the system level (Tiers 2–4) by the conflictoring protocol, using multiple agents with incommensurable evaluation criteria to make conformism strictly dominated.

Elinor Ostrom's work on governing the commons demonstrated that communities can develop sustainable self-governance institutions for common-pool resources. Her eight design principles, such as clearly defined boundaries, monitoring, and graduated sanctions, have been empirically validated for local commons like fisheries and irrigation systems. The connection to Private-Systemic Tension lies in the scale condition. Ostrom's framework implicitly assumes that the community can monitor the state of the commons. However, Axiom 2 of the Private-Systemic Tension theorem (System Independence) states that system welfare cannot be computed from bilateral party payoffs. At a global scale, this monitoring condition fails absolutely. No community can monitor the global atmosphere or international financial system by observing local transactions. The conflictoring protocol addresses this 'pre-Ostrom' problem by providing the external pressure and measurement infrastructure needed to transform Private-Systemic Tension games into a state where Ostrom's principles can then be applied. For intractability-class domains, the conflictoring protocol aims to achieve the institutional redesign that leads to stable, self-sustaining non-Private-Systemic Tension equilibria, analogous to the self-governance outcomes Ostrom described for local commons.

faq.q: The paper claims $85.0 trillion in annual welfare destruction. Is this figure reliable, and how can it be so high?
faq.a: The figure of $85.0 trillion in annual system welfare destruction is derived from the Systemic Asset Pricing Model catalog, which aggregates costs across 59 market-failure domains plus controls of the global economy. This figure is not a speculative estimate but is robustly established through 100,000 Monte Carlo draws, confirming its structural nature. The magnitude is high because it encompasses a comprehensive range of welfare costs, including mortality (quantified using the Value of a Statistical Life methodology), defensive spending (Cleanup Gross Domestic Product), suppressed economic productivity, and the present value of deferred catastrophic risks that materialize over decades or centuries. For example, the long-term health impacts of pollution, the economic costs of climate change, and the societal burden of issues like human trafficking or cybercrime, when aggregated and properly valued, sum to this substantial amount. The paper explicitly states that the welfare beta (βW) for the worst-performing domains, such as firearms (51.0), cybercrime (31.1), and human trafficking (22.62), demonstrates that the destruction is not incidental but structural. This means that for every dollar of revenue generated in these sectors, many more dollars of system welfare are destroyed. The methodology is designed to capture the full scope of externalized costs that traditional economic accounting often overlooks, making the figure a comprehensive measure of the societal burden imposed by Private-Systemic Tension games.

faq.q: The paper states that Coase, Pigou, and Ostrom fail. Does this mean these foundational economic theories are fundamentally flawed?
faq.a: The paper does not claim that Coase's bargaining theorem, Pigou's corrective tax, or Ostrom's common-pool resource governance are fundamentally flawed in their original domains of applicability. Instead, it precisely identifies the boundary conditions under which these frameworks fail, specifically at the global scale where Private-Systemic Tension games are most prevalent. The failure is not a refutation of their validity but a scope limitation. Each framework requires a condition that Axiom 2 of the Private-Systemic Tension theorem, System Independence, prevents from holding. Coase's theorem, for instance, assumes zero transaction costs and well-defined property rights, which are absent for global commons. More critically, it requires parties to price the externality, which Axiom 2 states is uncomputable from bilateral information. Pigou's tax requires a government to compute the marginal social cost, which is impossible for cross-jurisdictional externalities. Ostrom's self-governance relies on a community's ability to monitor the commons, which is not feasible for global systems. The paper argues that these frameworks fail not because they are wrong, but because they require measuring or pricing system welfare, which Axiom 2 establishes as formally uncomputable from the information available to bilateral parties. The conflictoring protocol is presented not as an alternative to these theories, but as an institutional architecture designed to make system welfare measurable and accountable, thereby creating the conditions under which these foundational frameworks might then become applicable or their underlying goals achieved.

methodology:
The paper's methodology is rooted in game theory, institutional economics, and welfare economics, extending foundational results to global system welfare games. It begins by formalizing the Private-Systemic Tension theorem, which defines the structural conditions under which bilateral transactions lead to externalized system welfare destruction. This theorem is built upon three axioms: Overlapping Interests (Axiom 1), System Independence (Axiom 2), and System Dependence (Axiom 3). The proof of the theorem is direct, demonstrating that the welfare destruction is a stable equilibrium outcome under these axioms.

The paper draws heavily on the Systemic Asset Pricing Model working paper series, particularly Postnieks (2026b) for the formal Private-Systemic Tension game structure, and Postnieks (2026c) for the aggregate welfare destruction figures and the Reform Dividend arithmetic. The $85.0 trillion annual system welfare destruction figure is an aggregation across 59 market-failure domains plus controls, calculated from four primary channels: Value of a Statistical Life (mortality), Cleanup Gross Domestic Product (defensive spending), Productivity (suppressed output), and Future (deferred damage). The welfare beta (βW) metric, defined as ΔW/Π (annual system welfare destruction divided by annual industry revenue), is central to quantifying the problem and identifying high-risk domains.

A key methodological step is the demonstration of the structural isomorphism between the Private-Systemic Tension game and Prat's (2005) conformism problem. This connection allows the paper to apply the resolution developed in Postnieks (2026a), which introduced a multi-audience common agency architecture to resolve organizational-level Private-Systemic Tension games. This resolution is then extended to system-level games through the six-agent conflictoring protocol.

The conflictoring protocol's effectiveness is formalized through a proposition based on three conditions: Coverage (C1), Incommensurability (C2), and Escalation (C3). The proof sketch for this proposition demonstrates that as the number of simultaneously active agents increases, the expected costs imposed on Private-Systemic Tension-maintaining strategies eventually exceed the bilateral private gain, making Private-Systemic Tension conformism strictly dominated.

Empirical validation and illustration are provided through three detailed case studies: the Montreal Protocol, OECD Pillar Two, and leaded gasoline. These cases serve to map agent activation, assess the k versus k* status, and extract key structural lessons regarding the speed and conditions of Private-Systemic Tension transformation. The paper also introduces the Disclosure Futility Theorem (Postnieks 2026g), drawing on behavioral game theory results from Bernheim and Rangel (2004), Gul and Pesendorfer (2001), and Lipnowski and Mathevet (2018), along with empirical data from various regulatory disclosures.

The paper acknowledges limitations of prior frameworks (Coase, Pigou, Ostrom) not as flaws in their domain, but as scope limitations at global scale due to Axiom 2. The Monte Carlo methodology, involving 100,000 draws, is used to establish the robustness of welfare destruction estimates, particularly for high-βW domains. However, specific details on Monte Carlo distribution types, seeds, or sensitivity analysis results are not available in the source paper. What would change the result is a fundamental alteration of the axioms, for example, if system welfare could be made computable from bilateral payoffs (breaking Axiom 2) or if agent activity ceased to degrade the shared system (breaking Axiom 3). The paper argues that such changes are not achievable through bilateral mechanisms or simple policy interventions, necessitating the multi-agent architectural approach of the conflictoring protocol.

The paper's research and writing synthesize insights from a broad bibliography, including works on common agency (Bernheim and Whinston 1986), herd behavior (Scharfstein and Stein 1990), and institutional economics (North 1990, Acemoglu and Robinson 2012), positioning its contributions within a rich academic context.

timeline.event: Arthur Pigou publishes The Economics of Welfare, formalizing the concept of corrective taxation for externalities.
timeline.significance: Pigou's work establishes a foundational framework for government intervention in markets to address social costs, but the paper argues it fails at global scale due to jurisdictional arbitrage and the uncomputability of global externalities (Axiom 2).

timeline.event: Leaded gasoline is first sold commercially.
timeline.significance: Marks the beginning of a 98-year Private-Systemic Tension game for leaded gasoline, characterized by delayed agent activation and persistent welfare destruction, illustrating k ≪ k*.

timeline.event: Ronald Coase publishes The Problem of Social Cost, introducing the Coase theorem.
timeline.significance: Coase's theorem provides an alternative to Pigouvian taxes, suggesting private bargaining can achieve efficient outcomes. The paper argues it fails at global scale due to ill-defined property rights, prohibitive transaction costs, and Axiom 2's uncomputability of system welfare.

timeline.event: Molina and Rowland publish their hypothesis on chlorofluorocarbons destroying the ozone layer.
timeline.significance: This scientific disclosure acts as an early 'whistleblower' event, making system welfare destruction partially observable and initiating the process that would lead to the Montreal Protocol.

timeline.event: The Environmental Protection Agency issues its first lead phasedown rules for gasoline in the United States.
timeline.significance: Fifty years after commercial introduction, this marks the first significant regulatory action against leaded gasoline in the United States, indicating the slow activation of Agent 3 (Regulator).

timeline.event: The United States Environmental Protection Agency bans chlorofluorocarbon aerosol sprays.
timeline.significance: An early regulatory action (Agent 3) in the chlorofluorocarbon case, demonstrating national efforts before international coordination.

timeline.event: The British Antarctic Survey observes the Antarctic ozone hole.
timeline.significance: This empirical evidence serves as a powerful, undeniable 'whistleblower' event, making the system welfare destruction (ozone depletion) visible and accelerating the need for international action.

timeline.event: The Montreal Protocol on Substances that Deplete the Ozone Layer is signed.
timeline.significance: A landmark supranational agreement (Agent 6) that rapidly transformed the chlorofluorocarbon Private-Systemic Tension game, demonstrating the power of simultaneous agent activation (k ≥ k*).

timeline.event: DuPont announces an accelerated phase-out of chlorofluorocarbons.
timeline.significance: This investor-driven decision (Agent 5) to switch to hydrofluorocarbon alternatives, driven by commercial opportunity and repricing of stranded-asset risk, demonstrates a critical agent activation shortly after the Montreal Protocol.

timeline.event: Elinor Ostrom publishes Governing the Commons, challenging conventional wisdom on common-pool resource management.
timeline.significance: Ostrom's work provides a third major framework for addressing externalities, focusing on community self-governance. The paper identifies its limitation at global scale due to Axiom 2's uncomputability of system welfare.

timeline.event: The complete United States ban on leaded gasoline becomes effective.
timeline.significance: Seventy-three years after commercial introduction, this legislative action (Agent 4) marks a significant step in the leaded gasoline phase-out, highlighting the long delay in structural reform.

timeline.event: The United Nations Environment Programme launches the Global Initiative to Eliminate Leaded Petrol.
timeline.significance: Marks the activation of Agent 6 (Supranational) for leaded gasoline, 79 years after its commercial introduction, demonstrating the necessity of international coordination for global phase-out.

timeline.event: Andrea Prat publishes The Wrong Kind of Transparency, formalizing the conformism problem.
timeline.significance: Prat's theorem establishes that transparency can induce agents to conform to observable principal preferences rather than maximize welfare. This paper identifies the Private-Systemic Tension game as structurally isomorphic to this problem.

timeline.event: DuPont settles with approximately 3,500 plaintiffs for $671 million in PFAS litigation.
timeline.significance: An early significant plaintiff action (Agent 2) in the PFAS domain, indicating the slow but growing activation of litigation as a Private-Systemic Tension-breaking mechanism.

timeline.event: Algeria, the last country, bans leaded gasoline.
timeline.significance: The global phase-out of leaded gasoline is completed 98 years after its commercial introduction, serving as a stark example of the prolonged timeline when agent activation is slow and serial.

timeline.event: The Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development Pillar Two framework becomes operational.
timeline.significance: This framework demonstrates Agent 6 (Supranational) as a necessary and sufficient precondition for Pigouvian taxation of cross-border externalities, successfully addressing corporate profit shifting and generating substantial tax revenue.

timeline.event: Erik Postnieks publishes The Conflictoring Protocol: Private-Systemic Tension → Non-Private-Systemic Tension Game Transformation.
timeline.significance: This paper formalizes the six-agent conflictoring protocol as a multi-principal architecture to transform Private-Systemic Tension games, extending the resolution of Prat's conformism problem to system-level welfare games and providing a comprehensive institutional hierarchy for global system welfare governance.

literatureContext:
This paper is situated at the intersection of game theory, institutional economics, and welfare economics, extending and integrating several foundational results to address large-scale global system welfare destruction. It directly engages with and builds upon the work of Ronald Coase (1960), Arthur Pigou (1920), and Elinor Ostrom (1990), demonstrating a shared structural failure in their application to global Private-Systemic Tension games.

Conventional analysis of externalities typically presents Coase, Pigou, and Ostrom as alternative solutions. Coase's bargaining theorem suggests that private parties can negotiate efficient outcomes in the absence of transaction costs and with well-defined property rights. Pigou's corrective tax proposes government intervention to internalize social costs. Ostrom's work highlights community-based self-governance for common-pool resources. This paper fundamentally differs by arguing that these three frameworks fail for the same underlying reason at global scale: Axiom 2 of the Private-Systemic Tension theorem (System Independence) prevents the welfare pricing or measurement that each framework requires. Coase needs pricing for bargaining, Pigou needs pricing for the tax rate, and Ostrom needs measurement for monitoring. This unified critique of their limitations in Private-Systemic Tension domains is a significant departure from conventional treatments.

The paper also makes a crucial connection to Andrea Prat's (2005) conformism problem, a recognized impossibility in accountability theory. Prat's work showed that transparency, when an agent's actions are observed by a single principal, can lead to conformist behavior rather than welfare maximization. This paper establishes a structural isomorphism between the Private-Systemic Tension game and Prat's conformism problem, arguing that industries in Private-Systemic Tension domains conform to the preferences of their bilateral counterparties (the single audience) while externalizing system welfare costs. This extends the scope of Prat's result from organizational governance to system-level economic interactions.

The resolution to Prat's conformism problem, developed in Postnieks (2026a) through a multi-audience common agency architecture, is then extended by this paper to system-level games. This is a significant contribution to the governance literature, as it provides a constructive resolution to a problem previously treated as an impossibility. The six-agent conflictoring protocol is the system-level manifestation of this multi-audience architecture, designed to make Private-Systemic Tension conformism strictly dominated.

Key citations that inform this work include: Coase (1960) and Stigler (1966) for the Coase theorem; Pigou (1920) for corrective taxation; Ostrom (1990) for commons governance; Prat (2005), Scharfstein and Stein (1990), and Levy (2007) for the conformism problem and herding; Bernheim and Whinston (1986) for common agency; and behavioral economics work by Bernheim and Rangel (2004), Gul and Pesendorfer (2001), and Lipnowski and Mathevet (2018) for the Disclosure Futility Theorem. The paper also draws on the broader institutional economics literature (North 1990, Acemoglu and Robinson 2012) to explain the persistence of welfare-destroying institutional equilibria.

This paper's contribution is to synthesize these disparate strands of economic thought into a unified framework for understanding and transforming Private-Systemic Tension games. It moves beyond identifying problems to proposing a formal, architecturally driven solution, offering a diagnostic tool and a prescriptive protocol for achieving large-scale system welfare improvements. The framework's emphasis on agent diversity, incommensurability, and escalation distinguishes it from prior analyses that often focus on single-agent interventions or static enforcement mechanisms.
